output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.7, "R1.3": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.9, "R1.6": 0.8, "R1.7": 0.4, "R1.4": 0.3}}

Summary: Reorganized the section into a coherent Setup → Related Work → Results → Analysis flow, moved the misplaced related-work paragraph out of the results stream, tightened prose, surfaced the baselines and ablation more clearly, and flagged several gaps the authors should fill.

---

## Revised draft

**Related work.** Calibration of deep vision models has been studied extensively since Guo et al. (2017) showed that modern CNNs are systematically overconfident relative to their ResNet-era predecessors. Standard post-hoc remedies include temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017), vector and matrix scaling (Kull et al., 2019), and Dirichlet calibration (Kull et al., 2019). Training-time alternatives include focal-loss objectives (Mukhoti et al., 2020), label smoothing (Müller et al., 2019), and deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017). A more recent thread asks whether *linguistic* confidence expressions emitted by vision-language models correlate with answer correctness (e.g., Tian et al., 2023; Xiong et al., 2024); we extend this line by treating verbal confidence as the primary calibration signal rather than an auxiliary one.

**Experimental setup.** We evaluate verbal-confidence elicitation on three benchmarks: ImageNet-1k, ImageNet-C (severities 1–5), and ObjectNet. The base model is BLIP-2 (ViT-G + FlanT5-XL), fine-tuned for three epochs on a 50k-image VQA-style calibration corpus in which each example pairs an image, a class-prediction question, and a verbal confidence label drawn from {*very low, low, medium, high, very high*}. We report top-1 accuracy and expected calibration error (ECE, 15 equal-width bins); reliability diagrams are deferred to Appendix X. We compare against two baselines applied to the same BLIP-2 backbone: (i) raw logit confidence (max softmax over class tokens) and (ii) temperature scaling fitted on a held-out validation split.

**In-distribution calibration.** Table 2 reports the headline numbers. Verbal-confidence elicitation attains an ECE of 4.7% on ImageNet-1k, compared with 12.1% for raw logit confidence and 5.3% for temperature scaling — a 0.6-point absolute improvement over the strongest post-hoc baseline.

**Distribution shift.** Verbal confidences remain informative under shift. On ObjectNet, ECE rises only to 7.9%, while logit-based confidence degrades to 21.4%. Under ImageNet-C, average ECE across severities 1–5 is 9.2%, a 4.5-point degradation relative to clean ImageNet. Inspecting the predicted-label distribution, we find that the model preferentially shifts probability mass toward the *medium* verbal label as corruption severity increases, rather than collapsing onto a single bin — consistent with graceful uncertainty growth rather than miscalibrated overconfidence.

**Ablation.** A small-scale ablation (Table 3) removes the explanatory rationale prompt prefix and finds ECE rises to 6.8%, indicating that the rationale step contributes roughly 2 points of calibration improvement on ImageNet-1k.

---

## Issues the authors should address

1. **Missing baseline coverage.** The related-work paragraph lists matrix scaling, Dirichlet calibration, focal loss, and ensembles, but only temperature scaling and raw logits appear in Table 2. Either run at least one training-time baseline (focal loss is cheap) and one richer post-hoc baseline (Dirichlet), or explicitly justify the omission. As written, the comparison set is narrower than the literature review implies.

2. **ImageNet-C baseline numbers are missing.** The text gives 9.2% average ECE for the verbal method on ImageNet-C but does not state what logit confidence or temperature scaling do under the same shift. Without those numbers, the "robustness under shift" claim rests only on ObjectNet.

3. **The 0.6-point gain over temperature scaling is small.** A single-seed 4.7% vs. 5.3% gap may be within noise. Report seeds / confidence intervals, or at least a bootstrap on the test set; otherwise the headline result is hard to defend.

4. **Accuracy is mentioned but never reported.** Top-1 is listed as a primary metric — does verbal-confidence fine-tuning hurt classification accuracy relative to the off-the-shelf BLIP-2? This matters because a method that improves ECE by degrading accuracy is less compelling.

5. **Ablation is underspecified.** "Removes the explanatory rationale prompt prefix" needs the actual prompt strings (in the appendix is fine) and ideally a second ablation that varies the verbal-label cardinality (5 vs. 3 vs. 7 bins). "About 2 points" is also asymmetric — it is 2.1 points; just say so.

6. **Mapping from verbal labels to probabilities is not described.** ECE requires a scalar confidence. How are {*very low … very high*} mapped to numbers — uniform spacing on [0,1], learned, or elicited from human raters? This is a load-bearing design choice and should appear in the setup paragraph.

7. **"VQA-style calibration corpus" provenance.** Where do the gold verbal-confidence labels come from? Model self-reports, crowd annotations, or derived from a teacher model's correctness? This determines whether the method learns calibration or memorizes a labeling scheme.

8. **Citation hygiene.** Kull et al. (2019) is cited for both vector/matrix scaling and Dirichlet calibration — these are two different papers (Kull et al., NeurIPS 2019 covers Dirichlet; "Beyond temperature scaling" is Kull et al., NeurIPS 2019 as well, but the references should disambiguate). Label smoothing and deep ensembles are listed without citations.

9. **Severity-wise breakdown.** Reporting only the 1–5 average on ImageNet-C hides whether the verbal method degrades gracefully or cliff-edges at severity 4–5. A per-severity table or plot would strengthen the "graceful shift toward *medium*" claim, which currently is asserted rather than shown.

---

Uncertainty: the suggested citations for Müller et al. (2019), Lakshminarayanan et al. (2017), Tian et al. (2023), and Xiong et al. (2024) are standard-canon references for those topics but I did not verify exact titles/venues against a database — please confirm before inclusion. The note about two distinct Kull et al. (2019) papers is based on my recollection of the calibration literature and should be checked against the actual bibliography.
